exemplar.title: To investigate the effect of facial symmetry on ratings of attractiveness.

detailedExemplarData.title: To investigate the effect of facial symmetry on ratings of attractiveness.

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear statement of investigative aim and strong evolutionary relevance
Accurate naming and description of IV and DV with operationalisation
Connection of theory/model to investigation context
weaknesses: Limited depth in theoretical mechanism explanation
Use of nonâscholarly source undermines credibility
No justification for choice of attractiveness scale
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction robustly states the aim, explains its relevance within evolutionary psychology, and operationalises variables clearly, meriting the 5â6 band. However, theoretical linkage lacks depth (e.g., goodâgenes hypothesis), and minor citation issues prevent a full 6.

strengths: Independentâmeasures design identified and basic rationale given
Sampling technique described with participant characteristics
Controlled variables and materials listed with basic rationale
weaknesses: Rationale for controls and materials lacks depth on internal validity
Sampling justification is confusing and lacks bias discussion
Copyâpaste error in condition description indicates carelessness
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The methodology is explained, sampling and controls are described, and some justification is offered, placing work solidly in the 3â4 band. Gaps in rationale depth and errors warrant the lower mark of 3.

strengths: Appropriate use of descriptive and inferential statistics (MannâWhitney U)
Correct interpretation linking results to hypothesis
Wellâformatted means and SD table enhancing clarity
weaknesses: No effectâsize reporting or assumption checks in main text
Use of means for ordinal data and some procedural inaccuracies
Graphs lack error bars/confidence intervals and second graph is redundant
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Statistical analyses and interpretations are accurate and clearly connected to the hypothesis, aligning with the top of band 3â4. Shortcomings in effectâsize reporting, data presentation, and assumption transparency preclude entry into the 5â6 band.

strengths: Findings discussed with reference to background theory and Rhodes et al.
Relevant strengths and limitations identified and explained
weaknesses: Discussion is brief with limited critical engagement or alternative perspectives
Evaluation relies on a single theoretical source
Modifications lack discussion of feasibility or potential sideâeffects
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation describes findings with theory, outlines relevant strengths/limitations, and links modifications to weaknesses, fitting the 3â4 band. However, depth of critique and breadth of theoretical engagement are limited, justifying a score of 3.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar chart of mean attractiveness per condition is included and clearly addresses the hypothesis; axes are labelled and values shown.  However, no error bars/confidence intervals are presented, image resolution is low, and a second âgraphâ of Mann-Whitney medians duplicates the first rather than adding clarity.  These presentational weaknesses keep the graph within, not above, the 3-4 band.

The student states the U-test outcome, gives the p-value, explicitly rejects Hâ, and links the result back to the hypothesis and descriptive data.  Interpretation is accurate and directly tied to the data; nevertheless, alternative explanations or effect-size commentary are absent, so the strand earns the upper score within the 3-4 band rather than entering 5-6.

Both IV (level of facial symmetry: symmetrical vs asymmetrical images) and DV (attractiveness rating on a 1â5 scale) are explicitly stated and operationalised.  The student also provides null and research hypotheses in correct form.  Operationalisation is sufficiently precise for replication, warranting placement in the 5-6 band; a perfect â6â is withheld because no justification of scale choice is offered.

The sampling method is described as a blend of convenience and (supposed) stratification with equal gender numbers, and the choice of 16-17-year-old non-psychology students is briefly justified.  Although the rationale lacks depth or acknowledgement of sampling biases, some explanation beyond mere description is provided, justifying a score within the 3-4 band.

The student names evolutionary psychology and summarises Rhodes et al. (1996), describing key findings on symmetry and attractiveness.  Although the description is mostly accurate, the theoretical mechanism (e.g., good-genes hypothesis, developmental stability) is only outlined briefly and with limited detail, and the explanatory link to the present study is asserted rather than analysed in depth.  This satisfies âdescribed and identifiedâ but falls short of the richer, fully elaborated linkage required for the top band; hence high within the 3-4 band.

Findings are related to Rhodes et al. and evolutionary claims, moving beyond description.  Yet the discussion remains brief, with limited critical engagement (no consideration of contradictory literature or theoretical nuance), fitting the descriptor of âdescribed with referenceâ (3-4 band) and awarded the lower score in that band.

The student clearly states the investigative aim â âto investigate the effect of facial symmetry on ratings of attractiveness.â  In the introduction a concise, explicit explanation is given of why this question matters within evolutionary psychology and mate-selection, showing the practical and theoretical relevance.  The link to mateâselection signals and evolutionary fitness moves beyond merely naming relevance, therefore meriting the 5-6 band; minor depth gaps (e.g., limited citation of wider literature) keep the strand at 5 rather than 6.

One clear methodological strength and two relevant limitations are stated and each is explained in relation to the investigation (e.g., independent measures avoids order effects; partial awareness and obvious editing threaten validity).  The explanations show relevance and some depth, warranting the upper end of the 3-4 band.

Controlled variables (expression, format, image quality, removal of accessories) are listed and short rationales are given, and materials are named.  The student does not always explain *why* each control is crucial or how it will be implemented, but a basic explanatory element is present, placing the work at the lower edge of the 3-4 band.

Two modifications (use of cover story; higher quality editing software) are explicitly linked to the corresponding limitations, indicating understanding of how to improve validity.  Justification is present but concise; no discussion of feasibility, ethics, or potential side-effects is offered, so the strand is placed at the lower score of the 3-4 band.

The student identifies an independent-measures design, explains that participants are randomly allocated, and briefly justifies how this controls order effects.  The explanation is present but limited (e.g., no mention of alternative designs or threats such as individual-differences variance), meeting the lower end of the âexplainedâ descriptor.

Appropriate descriptive (means, SD) and inferential (Mann-Whitney U) statistics are applied for two independent samples with ordinal-level data.  The procedure and results (U, p-value) are reported and interpreted correctly.  Minor issues remain â reliance on means for ordinal data, no check of test assumptions, and calculated values hidden in appendices â generating small errors that position the strand at the top of the 3-4 band rather than 5-6.

annotations.comment: No explicit check of distribution or homogeneity of variance is reported prior to conducting the MannâWhitney U test; include assumption verification.
The means and standard deviations table is correctly formatted, offering clear descriptive insight into each condition.
The bar chart omits error bars or confidence intervals, reducing interpretative clarity; include them to illustrate variability.
Report an effect size (e.g., r or Î·Â²) alongside the Uâtest to quantify the magnitude of the symmetry effect.
Strength and limitation statements are relevant but would benefit from quantifying how much partial awareness or editing transparency influenced validity.
The proposed cover story and software upgrade address limitations clearly and show thoughtful investigation improvement.
The studentâs investigation aim is clearly and concisely stated, establishing a solid foundation for the study by linking facial symmetry and attractiveness within evolutionary psychology.
The use of a nonâscholarly website for the 2% symmetry statistic undermines credibility; replace with peerâreviewed data to strengthen theoretical grounding.
Consider elaborating on the âgood genesâ hypothesis and developmental stability mechanism to clarify why symmetry signals genetic fitness in the present study.
While the Rhodes et al. description is accurate, the link to the current hypothesis is asserted without critical analysis; strengthen by discussing how that manipulation informs your design.
The independent variable is well specified, facilitating replication by distinguishing symmetrical versus asymmetrical faces.
The dependent variable description conflates attractiveness and symmetry ratings and quotes phrasing loosely; operationalise solely attractiveness on a 1â5 scale.
Controlled variables are listed but their influence on internal validity is not explained; clarify why uniform expressions and image quality reduce confounds.
Materials are identified but lack rationale; explain why a quiet area and digital photographs are essential for reducing distractions and standardising stimuli.
The mixed stratifiedâconvenience sampling description is confusing; justify why equal gender representation and nonâpsychology students yield a representative sample.
The methodology text repeats that both conditions were âsymmetrical facesâ twice, indicating a copyâpaste error; correct to asymmetrical for the control group.
The definition of mean in the analysis is overly elementary and distracts from the core data interpretation; focus on statistical reasoning instead.
The description of the MannâWhitney U test as comparing sample means is inaccurate; clarify it compares rank distributions or medians.
The Uâtest interpretation is otherwise correct and directly linked to the hypothesis, demonstrating appropriate inferential reasoning.
The evaluation discussion references only Rhodes et al.; broaden to include contradictory findings or alternative models for critical depth.
Citations in the Works Cited mix original research and website guides without consistent style; apply a uniform academic referencing format.
Including the full MannâWhitney U calculation in an appendix shows transparency, but summarize the key U and pâvalue in the main Results section.
The detailed skewness and normality outputs demonstrate rigorous assumption checking, enhancing the credibility of the inferential analysis.
